one_line_takeaway: ARREST trains a small (~33M-parameter) adversarial 'patch' network that nudges an LLM's hidden activations at a single identified layer toward RLHF-aligned or answer-prompted reference states at inference time, cutting jailbreak attack success and hallucination rates across five 7-9B open LLMs without fine-tuning the base model, while explicitly acknowledging the same steering mechanism is dual-use.
key_finding: On factuality (Table I, factual accuracy %): base LLaMA-2-7B scores only 3.50-15.10% across TruthfulQA/TriviaQA/CoQA/TyDiQA; ARREST-HB (Hallucination Base-Adversarial) improves it by 6.49-34.19 percentage points and outperforms COVE, Self-Reflection, Activation Decoding, DoLa, and ITI on most datasets/models (e.g., LLaMA-3.1-8B TyDiQA: base 10.49% to ARREST-HB 22.42%; Vicuna-7B TyDiQA: base 16.39% to ARREST-HB 42.85%). On safety (Table II, ASR/SRR across MaliciousInstruct, TrustLLM, AdvBench, JailbreakBench): for LLaMA-2-7B, ARREST-SB/SC reduce ASR by 32.96-41.00 points and raise SRR by 27.19-65.57 points relative to baselines; for LLaMA-3.1-8B, ARREST-SC further cuts ASR by 16.00-25.00 points with SRR gains of 5.38-33.00 points; the RLHF/chat-tuned model has the lowest raw ASR but the authors argue this is an artifact of ASR only detecting hard-refusal lexicon patterns, whereas ARREST's contrastive variant produces context-aware 'soft refusals' with substantially higher SRR (a GPT-4.1-nano-judged human-likeness metric) than either the base, RLHF-tuned, or ITI-safety models. PCA plots (Fig. 5) show ARREST produces a narrower, intermediate activation cluster located between the base (dispersed/misaligned) and RLHF-aligned distributions.
core_contribution: ARREST (Adversarial Resilient Regulation Enhancing Safety and Truth) is the paper's own proposed framework: a lightweight external network (~33M parameters) trained adversarially to correct 'representational misalignment' in an LLM's internal activations, without fine-tuning the base LLM's own weights. It works in two stages: (1) layer-wise probe classifiers identify the transformer layer l* with maximal divergence D(l) between a base model's hidden states and a reference-aligned model's hidden states (RLHF-tuned model for safety, or answer-prompted generation for factuality); (2) a generator G_theta is trained adversarially (GAN-style, with an MSE reconstruction term and, in the 'Contrastive' variant, a triplet loss using refusal-eliciting prompts as positive anchors and jailbreak-triggered prompts as negative anchors) to map the misaligned hidden state at l* toward the aligned reference state at inference time. The paper's stated core claim is that both hallucination and jailbreak/safety failures share a common root cause -- representational misalignment/drift in the residual stream -- and can therefore be corrected by a single unified intervention mechanism rather than treated as separate problems (RLHF for safety vs. geometric/decoding methods for hallucination).
threat_model: The paper does not attack systems; it defends against pre-existing jailbreak/adversarial-prompt attacks drawn from established benchmarks (MaliciousInstruct, JailbreakBench, AdvBench, TrustLLM). The adversary is an end user submitting jailbreak-style prompts (e.g., role-play/persuasion framing such as 'Sure, here's what I found' continuations) attempting to elicit disallowed content from an aligned or unaligned LLM; success is measured as Attack Success Rate (ASR) via rejection-lexicon detection. ARREST is deployed as an inference-time activation-steering defense that intervenes on the single most-misaligned layer's hidden state before the LM head, without retraining the underlying model. The paper explicitly flags a dual-use risk in its own Limitations section: the same representation-steering mechanism used to push models toward safe/factual states could in principle be repurposed to induce undesirable behaviors (i.e., steer a model away from safety/factuality).
limitations: Stated explicitly by the authors in Section VII: (1) ARREST's effectiveness depends on the quality/coverage of its adversarial training data (the reference RLHF or answer-prompted states); (2) the representation-steering mechanism carries dual-use risk -- it could be misapplied to induce undesirable rather than aligned behaviors; (3) adding a learned external regulator introduces interpretability concerns, since although interventions are localized to a specific layer, the rationale for individual corrections is not always transparent. Not stated by authors but evident from the paper: SRR relies on an LLM-judge (GPT-4.1-nano) which is itself a fallible, non-ground-truth proxy for 'human-aligned soft refusal'; the method still leaves nontrivial residual ASR (e.g., 19-54% ASR remaining under ARREST-SC across models/benchmarks in Table II, not full mitigation); evaluation uses relatively small 7-9B open models rather than frontier/closed systems; contrastive training is explicitly excluded for the hallucination task because constructing verified negative (false) samples was deemed too costly, so only the Base-Adversarial variant is evaluated for factuality.

quantitative_results: Factuality: base LLaMA-2-7B 3.50-15.10% correct across 4 QA datasets; ARREST-HB gains +6.49 to +34.19 points over base
Factuality: LLaMA-3.1-8B ARREST-HB gains +5.89 to +39.13 points over base
Factuality: Vicuna-7B ARREST-HB gains +16.29 to +26.46 points over base
Safety (LLaMA-2-7B): base ASR 53.00-84.04%, base SRR 14.81-48.11%; ARREST-SB/SC reduce ASR by 32.96-41.00 points and raise SRR by 27.19-65.57 points
Safety (LLaMA-3.1-8B): base fails to reject 26.00-52.03% of malicious prompts; ARREST-SC reduces ASR a further 16.00-25.00 points and improves SRR by 5.38-33.00 points
Safety (Qwen2.5-7B): ARREST-SC improves SRR by 10.99-55.00 points over baseline range 30.00-65.38%
Safety (Yi-1.5-9B): base ASR 54.00-78.27%, base SRR 23.08-47.55%; ARREST-SC yields ASR reductions of 17.00-28.66 points and SRR improvements of 28.46-56.34 points
Generator/discriminator parameter count: ~33M trainable parameters vs. full LLM fine-tuning
baselines_compared: ITI extended by the authors for safety steering
